exercises.title: Order the complexities
exercises.instructions:
Arrange these Big-O complexities from FASTEST (fewest operations) to SLOWEST for large n:

O(n²), O(1), O(n log n), O(log n), O(n), O(2ⁿ)
exercises.expected_solution:
FASTEST → SLOWEST:
1. O(1)       — Constant: always 1 operation regardless of n
   Example: array[i], HashMap.get(), stack.peek()

2. O(log n)   — Logarithmic: doubles input → +1 operation
   Example: binary search (n=1M → 20 steps)

3. O(n)       — Linear: doubles input → doubles operations
   Example: single for-loop over array

4. O(n log n) — Linearithmic: doubles input → just over doubles
   Example: merge sort, Collections.sort() (TimSort)

5. O(n²)      — Quadratic: doubles input → 4× operations
   Example: bubble sort, nested loop comparison

6. O(2ⁿ)      — Exponential: +1 to input → doubles operations
   Example: generating all subsets, brute-force recursion

At n = 1000:
  O(1)       →         1 operation
  O(log n)   →        10 operations
  O(n)       →     1,000 operations
  O(n log n) →    10,000 operations
  O(n²)      → 1,000,000 operations
  O(2ⁿ)      → 10^301 operations ← impossible

test_cases.expected:
FASTEST → SLOWEST:
1. O(1)       — Constant: always 1 operation regardless of n
   Example: array[i], HashMap.get(), stack.peek()

2. O(log n)   — Logarithmic: doubles input → +1 operation
   Example: binary search (n=1M → 20 steps)

3. O(n)       — Linear: doubles input → doubles operations
   Example: single for-loop over array

4. O(n log n) — Linearithmic: doubles input → just over doubles
   Example: merge sort, Collections.sort() (TimSort)

5. O(n²)      — Quadratic: doubles input → 4× operations
   Example: bubble sort, nested loop comparison

6. O(2ⁿ)      — Exponential: +1 to input → doubles operations
   Example: generating all subsets, brute-force recursion

At n = 1000:
  O(1)       →         1 operation
  O(log n)   →        10 operations
  O(n)       →     1,000 operations
  O(n log n) →    10,000 operations
  O(n²)      → 1,000,000 operations
  O(2ⁿ)      → 10^301 operations ← impossible

hints: O(1) is always fastest — independent of input size
log n grows much slower than n
n² grows much faster than n log n

exercises.title: Classify each operation
exercises.instructions:
Classify each Java operation as O(1), O(log n), O(n), or O(n log n):

A: `list.get(5)`
B: `Collections.sort(list)` — n elements
C: `list.contains(x)` — ArrayList
D: `map.get(key)` — HashMap
E: `map.get(key)` — TreeMap
F: `list.size()`
exercises.expected_solution:
A: O(1) — list.get(5)
   ArrayList: index access is direct memory offset
   list[5] = base_address + 5 × element_size
   Same time regardless of list size

B: O(n log n) — Collections.sort(list)
   Uses TimSort (merge sort + insertion sort hybrid)
   Lower bound for comparison-based sort is Ω(n log n)

C: O(n) — list.contains(x) on ArrayList
   Must scan from index 0 until x found or end
   Worst case: x not in list → all n elements checked

D: O(1) average — HashMap.get(key)
   Hash function → direct bucket lookup
   Average case: 1 hash + 1-2 comparisons
   Worst case: O(n) if all keys hash to same bucket (rare)

E: O(log n) — TreeMap.get(key)
   Red-Black Tree: balanced BST
   Tree height = O(log n) → each get traverses log n nodes
   Slower than HashMap but keys are always sorted

F: O(1) — list.size()
   ArrayList stores size as a field (updated on add/remove)
   No counting needed — just return the stored integer

test_cases.expected:
A: O(1) — list.get(5)
   ArrayList: index access is direct memory offset
   list[5] = base_address + 5 × element_size
   Same time regardless of list size

B: O(n log n) — Collections.sort(list)
   Uses TimSort (merge sort + insertion sort hybrid)
   Lower bound for comparison-based sort is Ω(n log n)

C: O(n) — list.contains(x) on ArrayList
   Must scan from index 0 until x found or end
   Worst case: x not in list → all n elements checked

D: O(1) average — HashMap.get(key)
   Hash function → direct bucket lookup
   Average case: 1 hash + 1-2 comparisons
   Worst case: O(n) if all keys hash to same bucket (rare)

E: O(log n) — TreeMap.get(key)
   Red-Black Tree: balanced BST
   Tree height = O(log n) → each get traverses log n nodes
   Slower than HashMap but keys are always sorted

F: O(1) — list.size()
   ArrayList stores size as a field (updated on add/remove)
   No counting needed — just return the stored integer

hints: ArrayList.get(i): direct index — O(1)
HashMap: hash function maps key to bucket — O(1) average
TreeMap: sorted tree — O(log n)

exercises.title: Simplify Big-O expressions
exercises.instructions:
Simplify each Big-O expression by dropping constants and non-dominant terms. Then write ONE Java statement that has that complexity.

1. O(3n + 100)
2. O(n² + 50n + 1000)
3. O(n³ + n² + n)
4. O(2ⁿ + n¹⁰⁰)
5. O(n log n + n)
exercises.starter_code: // Simplify and give a Java example for each
hints: Drop constants (3, 50, 1000): they don't affect growth
Dominant term = highest degree or fastest-growing
Exponential beats any polynomial

exercises.title: Find the Big-O of Flipkart search functions
hints: Linear search: O(n) — must visit each element in worst case
Array index: O(1) — no loop needed
Two different input sizes: O(n + m), not O(n)

exercises.title: Big-O of nested vs sequential loops
hints: Sequential: add complexities
Nested: multiply complexities
Inner loop that halves: log n iterations

exercises.title: Choose the better algorithm
exercises.instructions:
Zerodha has 5 million traders. For each scenario, choose Algorithm A or B and explain why:

1. Find if trader ID exists: A=linear scan O(n), B=HashMap O(1)
2. Get sorted leaderboard: A=insertion sort O(n²), B=merge sort O(n log n)
3. Get 3rd element of a list: A=LinkedList.get(2) O(n), B=ArrayList.get(2) O(1)
4. Count unique stocks traded: A=ArrayList+contains O(n²), B=HashSet+add O(n)

Write program that prints the choice and operation count for n=1,000,000.
hints: HashMap/HashSet: O(1) average for contains/get/add
ArrayList.get(i): O(1). LinkedList.get(i): O(n)
Always prefer lower Big-O for large inputs

exercises.title: Identify Big-O from code — Razorpay transaction audit
hints: i+1 to n inner loop: still O(n²) — n*(n-1)/2 ≈ n²/2 → drop constant
i /= 2: halving → O(log n)
HashSet operations inside a single loop: O(n) × O(1) = O(n)

exercises.title: Two solutions, same problem — find the better one
exercises.instructions:
Two solutions to: 'Find if any two numbers in Zerodha's trade list sum to target'.

Solution A (brute force): try every pair
Solution B (optimized): use a HashSet

Implement both. Compare their complexities. Test with n=100,000 and measure which is faster.
hints: HashSet.contains(): O(1) average — makes the whole loop O(n)
target - num: if this exists in the set, we found our pair
This is LeetCode #1 — Two Sum — asked in nearly every coding interview

exercises.title: Calculate operation count at different scales
exercises.instructions: Razorpay runs algorithms at different scales. For each complexity, calculate the exact operation count at n=10, n=1000, n=1,000,000. Print a table and identify which complexities become unusable.
hints: log₂(n) in Java: Math.log(n)/Math.log(2)
At n=1M: O(n²) = 10^12 — about 16 minutes at 10^9 ops/sec
Format output as a table for interview clarity

exercises.title: Trick question — what is the complexity?
exercises.expected_solution:
Trick 1: O(n) NOT O(n²)
  Outer: n iterations
  Inner: CONSTANT 5 iterations (not n)
  Total: n × 5 = 5n = O(n)
  Rule: if inner loop bound is a constant, not n → NOT O(n²)

Trick 2: O(n log n) NOT O(n²)
  Outer: i=1,2,4,8...n → log₂(n) iterations (doubles each time)
  Inner: n iterations
  Total: log(n) × n = O(n log n)
  Same as merge sort's outer merge levels × inner merge work

Trick 3: O(n²) — this one IS quadratic despite the triangle shape
  Total work = n + (n-1) + (n-2) + ... + 1 = n(n+1)/2
  n(n+1)/2 = n²/2 + n/2 → drop constant 1/2 and lower term
  Result: O(n²)
  Key: even 'triangular' nested loops = O(n²)

Trick 4: O(n) assuming println is O(1)
  System.out.println(i): prints the integer i
  Length of i's string representation: at most log₁₀(n) digits
  Technically O(n log n) if we count character writes!
  In interview practice: assume println is O(1)
  In precise analysis: could argue O(n log n) for printing all digits

test_cases.expected:
Trick 1: O(n) NOT O(n²)
  Outer: n iterations
  Inner: CONSTANT 5 iterations (not n)
  Total: n × 5 = 5n = O(n)
  Rule: if inner loop bound is a constant, not n → NOT O(n²)

Trick 2: O(n log n) NOT O(n²)
  Outer: i=1,2,4,8...n → log₂(n) iterations (doubles each time)
  Inner: n iterations
  Total: log(n) × n = O(n log n)
  Same as merge sort's outer merge levels × inner merge work

Trick 3: O(n²) — this one IS quadratic despite the triangle shape
  Total work = n + (n-1) + (n-2) + ... + 1 = n(n+1)/2
  n(n+1)/2 = n²/2 + n/2 → drop constant 1/2 and lower term
  Result: O(n²)
  Key: even 'triangular' nested loops = O(n²)

Trick 4: O(n) assuming println is O(1)
  System.out.println(i): prints the integer i
  Length of i's string representation: at most log₁₀(n) digits
  Technically O(n log n) if we count character writes!
  In interview practice: assume println is O(1)
  In precise analysis: could argue O(n log n) for printing all digits

hints: Inner loop constant bound: O(n) not O(n²)
Outer loop doubles: log n iterations
n(n+1)/2 = O(n²) — triangular sum is still quadratic

exercises.title: Benchmark O(n) vs O(n²) on Zerodha data
exercises.instructions:
Implement and benchmark two approaches to 'find duplicates' in a trade list:
1. Nested loop approach: O(n²)
2. HashSet approach: O(n)

Run on n = 1000, 10000, 100000. Print timing table. Show the speedup grows with n.
hints: seen.add() returns false if element already existed — elegant O(1) duplicate detection
LinkedHashSet preserves insertion order while deduplicating
Speedup should grow proportionally to n

exercises.title: Classify real Java API operations
exercises.instructions: Write ComplexityReference — a program that classifies the Big-O of 15 common Java operations, grouped by data structure. Use correct Big-O for each. Print as a formatted reference table.
hints: ArrayList.add() is amortized O(1) — resizing is rare
TreeMap uses Red-Black Tree: all operations O(log n)
PriorityQueue.peek() is O(1) — root is always min

exercises.title: Find Most Frequent Trade — O(n) solution
exercises.instructions:
Given n trade IDs (strings), find the most frequent one.

Constraint: Must be O(n) time complexity.

Example: trades = ["RELIANCE","TCS","RELIANCE","INFY","TCS","RELIANCE"]
Output: "RELIANCE" (appears 3 times)

Test with n=1,000,000 trades and verify it runs in under 1 second.
hints: HashMap.getOrDefault(key, default): elegant frequency counting
Two O(n) passes = O(2n) = O(n)
This pattern (count with HashMap, then scan) solves MANY frequency problems

exercises.title: Interview-style: improve O(n²) to O(n log n)
exercises.instructions:
Interviewer: 'Find the number of inversions in a trade price array. An inversion is a pair (i,j) where i<j but prices[i] > prices[j].'

Example: prices = [3,1,2] → 2 inversions: (3,1) and (3,2)

1. Implement the O(n²) brute force solution
2. The interviewer says 'Can you do better?' — explain O(n log n) approach using merge sort
3. Implement the O(n log n) solution

This is a real Flipkart interview question.
hints: During merge: if arr[i] > arr[j], then (mid-i+1) elements form inversions with arr[j]
Merge sort naturally counts inversions while sorting
This pattern (sorting to count) appears in many O(n log n) optimizations

exercises.title: Complete M29 T1 Big-O Synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete Big-O synthesis:
1. Implement solutions to the same problem at O(n²), O(n log n), O(n), O(1)
   Problem: Given sorted array, find if target exists
2. Benchmark all 4 at n=100,000
3. Print complexity table with actual timings
4. Verify all give same answer

Also implement: `classify(int n, long ops)` → returns Big-O class based on operation count.
hints: Binary search: lo + (hi-lo)/2 avoids integer overflow
classify(): compare ops to n, n*logN, n*n thresholds
O(log n) should be by far the fastest at n=100K
